title: The Science of Sleep: Why Your Brain Needs Rest

slides.heading: The Science of Sleep
slides.body: Why your brain desperately needs those 8 hours — and what happens when it doesn't get them

slides.heading: Sleep Is Not Passive
slides.body: Your brain is far from idle during sleep. Neurons fire, memories consolidate, and critical repair processes run in overdrive. Sleep is one of the most active and complex states your body enters every night.

slides.heading: The Two Types of Sleep
slides.body: Sleep cycles through REM (Rapid Eye Movement) and non-REM stages roughly every 90 minutes. Non-REM handles deep physical restoration, while REM is when vivid dreaming and memory processing peak.

slides.heading: Memory Consolidation
slides.body: During sleep, the hippocampus replays the day's events and transfers key information to long-term storage in the cortex. This is why pulling an all-nighter before an exam often backfires — you lose the consolidation window.

slides.heading: The Brain's Cleaning Crew
slides.body: The glymphatic system — your brain's waste-clearance network — is nearly 10x more active during sleep. It flushes out toxic proteins, including beta-amyloid, a key culprit in Alzheimer's disease.

slides.heading: Sleep and Emotional Regulation
slides.body: The amygdala, your brain's emotional alarm center, becomes 60% more reactive after just one night of poor sleep. Chronic sleep deprivation is strongly linked to anxiety, depression, and impaired decision-making.

slides.heading: Circadian Rhythm: Your Inner Clock
slides.body: A tiny brain region called the suprachiasmatic nucleus tracks light exposure and regulates your 24-hour sleep-wake cycle. Disrupting it — through shift work or jet lag — raises risks of metabolic disease and cancer.

slides.heading: How Much Do You Really Need?
slides.body: Adults need 7–9 hours per night, teenagers need 8–10, and newborns up to 17. Genetics play a role — roughly 3% of people carry a gene mutation that lets them thrive on 6 hours. You're probably not one of them.

slides.heading: The Cost of Sleep Debt
slides.body: After 17 hours awake, cognitive impairment equals a blood alcohol level of 0.05%. After 24 hours, it hits 0.10% — legally drunk in most countries. Sleep debt cannot be fully paid back with a single weekend of catch-up sleep.

slides.heading: Sleep Smarter Tonight
slides.body: Keep a consistent sleep schedule, dim lights an hour before bed, and keep your room cool. Your brain does its best work while you're unconscious — give it the time it needs.
